sections.heading: Slow Queries
sections.body:
To optimize your query performance, [review the best practices for query performance](query-best-practices). You can also
[analyze slow queries](analyze-slow-queries) and troubleshoot
slow operations executed on your clusters.

Use the following built-in tools:

- [Performance Advisor](performance-advisor) with
  the Performance Advisor.

- [monitor collection-level query latency](namespace-insights)
  with Namespace Insights.

- [Query Profiler](profile-database) with the Query
  Profiler.

- [Real-Time Performance Panel](real-time-metrics-status-tab)
  with the Real-Time Performance Panel.

sections.heading: Schema Design
sections.body:
To optimize your schema design, review our frequently used [schema design patterns](schema-design-patterns). You can also [improve your schema](schema-suggestions). Improve your schema based on
recommendations from the [Performance Advisor](performance-advisor) and the [Atlas UI](atlas-ui-dbs).

sections.heading: Alerts
sections.body:
To trigger alerts based on [alert conditions](alert-conditions)
and to help ensure cluster performance,
[configure your alert settings](alerts). You can
configure alerts based on specific conditions for your
databases, users, accounts, and more. When you resolve alerts, you
should fix the immediate problem, implement a long-term solution, and
monitor your progress.

Before you get started with alerts, review the [Alert Basics](alert-basics).

sections.heading: Deployment Metrics
sections.body:
To monitor your cluster performance, [performance metrics](monitor-cluster-metrics). View historical
throughput, performance, and utilization metrics for your databases. To
learn more, [Review Available Metrics](review-available-metrics).

sections.heading: Third-Party Integrations
sections.body:
To receive Atlas alerts in various
external monitoring services,
[Alert Webhooks](third-party-integrations).

sections.heading: MongoDB Logs
sections.body:
Atlas provides several methods for accessing your [log messages](manual/reference/log-messages) and [system event audit messages](manual/reference/audit-message).

- [log data](mongodb-logs) directly
  from the Atlas UI, API, or CLI for manual inspection and
  troubleshooting.

- [exporting Atlas logs to Amazon S3](export-logs-external-sinks) to AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) buckets.

- Use the Atlas Administration API to [pull log data](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-monitoring-and-logs)
  every 5 minutes or [pull monitoring data](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-monitoring-and-logs).

> **Note:** Atlas continues to support the following legacy methods for log and metric integration:
>
> - Use the legacy [Push Logs to AWS S3 bucket](mongodb-logs-push) feature to send logs
>   to an AWS (Amazon Web Services) S3 (Simple Storage Service) bucket. For new S3 (Simple Storage Service) integrations, we recommend using
>   [exporting Atlas logs to Amazon S3](export-logs-external-sinks) instead.
>
> - Configure push-based monitoring integrations
>   for Atlas with Datadog to send metrics.
>
> - Configure pull-based logging integrations with jSonar (which can push to other
>   services like Splunk) and SumoLogic.
